sourceRefs.fallbackText: Rob Pike 6 September 2011
sourceRefs.textQuote: Rob Pike 6 September 2011

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Reflection in computing is the ability of a program to examine its own structure, particularly through types; it’s a form of metaprogramming. It’s also a great source of confusion.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Reflection in computing is the ability of a program to examine its own structure, particularly through types; it’s a form of metaprogramming. It’s also a great source of confusion.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In this article we attempt to clarify things by explaining how reflection works in Go. Each language’s reflection model is different (and many languages don’t support it at all), but this article is about Go, so for the rest of this article the word “reflection” should be taken to mean “reflection in Go”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In this article we attempt to clarify things by explaining how reflection works in Go. Each language’s reflection model is different (and many languages don’t support it at all), but this article is about Go, so for the

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note added January 2022: This blog post was written in 2011 and predates parametric polymorphism (a.k.a. generics) in Go. Although nothing important in the article has become incorrect as a result of that development in the language, it has been tweaked in a few places to avoid confusing someone familiar with modern Go.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note added January 2022: This blog post was written in 2011 and predates parametric polymorphism (a.k.a. generics) in Go. Although nothing important in the article has become incorrect as a result of that development in

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Because reflection builds on the type system, let’s start with a refresher about types in Go.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Because reflection builds on the type system, let’s start with a refresher about types in Go.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go is statically typed. Every variable has a static type, that is, exactly one type known and fixed at compile time: int , float32 , *MyType , []byte , and so on. If we declare
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go is statically typed. Every variable has a static type, that is, exactly one type known and fixed at compile time: int , float32 , *MyType , []byte , and so on. If we declare

sourceRefs.fallbackText: type MyInt int var i int var j MyInt
sourceRefs.textQuote: type MyInt int var i int var j MyInt

sourceRefs.fallbackText: then i has type int and j has type MyInt . The variables i and j have distinct static types and, although they have the same underlying type, they cannot be assigned to one another without a conversion.
sourceRefs.textQuote: then i has type int and j has type MyInt . The variables i and j have distinct static types and, although they have the same underlying type, they cannot be assigned to one another without a conversion.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One important category of type is interface types, which represent fixed sets of methods. (When discussing reflection, we can ignore the use of interface definitions as constraints within polymorphic code.) An interface variable can store any concrete (non-interface) value as long as that value implements the interface’s methods. A well-known pair of example
sourceRefs.textQuote: One important category of type is interface types, which represent fixed sets of methods. (When discussing reflection, we can ignore the use of interface definitions as constraints within polymorphic code.) An interface

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Reader is the interface that wraps the basic Read method. type Reader interface { Read(p []byte) (n int, err error) } // Writer is the interface that wraps the basic Write method. type Writer interface { Write(p []byte) (n int, err error) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: // Reader is the interface that wraps the basic Read method. type Reader interface { Read(p []byte) (n int, err error) } // Writer is the interface that wraps the basic Write method. type Writer interface { Write(p []byt

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Any type that implements a Read (or Write ) method with this signature is said to implement io.Reader (or io.Writer ). For the purposes of this discussion, that means that a variable of type io.Reader can hold any value whose type has a Read method:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Any type that implements a Read (or Write ) method with this signature is said to implement io.Reader (or io.Writer ). For the purposes of this discussion, that means that a variable of type io.Reader can hold any value

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var r io.Reader r = os.Stdin r = bufio.NewReader(r) r = new(bytes.Buffer) // and so on
sourceRefs.textQuote: var r io.Reader r = os.Stdin r = bufio.NewReader(r) r = new(bytes.Buffer) // and so on

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It’s important to be clear that whatever concrete value r may hold, r ’s type is always io.Reader : Go is statically typed and the static type of r is io.Reader .
sourceRefs.textQuote: It’s important to be clear that whatever concrete value r may hold, r ’s type is always io.Reader : Go is statically typed and the static type of r is io.Reader .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An extremely important example of an interface type is the empty interface:
sourceRefs.textQuote: An extremely important example of an interface type is the empty interface:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: or its equivalent alias,
sourceRefs.textQuote: or its equivalent alias,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It represents the empty set of methods and is satisfied by any value at all, since every value has zero or more methods.
sourceRefs.textQuote: It represents the empty set of methods and is satisfied by any value at all, since every value has zero or more methods.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some people say that Go’s interfaces are dynamically typed, but that is misleading. They are statically typed: a variable of interface type always has the same static type, and even though at run time the value stored in the interface variable may change type, that value will always satisfy the interface.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Some people say that Go’s interfaces are dynamically typed, but that is misleading. They are statically typed: a variable of interface type always has the same static type, and even though at run time the value stored in

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We need to be precise about all this because reflection and interfaces are closely related.
sourceRefs.textQuote: We need to be precise about all this because reflection and interfaces are closely related.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Russ Cox has written a detailed blog post about the representation of interface values in Go. It’s not necessary to repeat the full story here, but a simplified summary is in order.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Russ Cox has written a detailed blog post about the representation of interface values in Go. It’s not necessary to repeat the full story here, but a simplified summary is in order.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A variable of interface type stores a pair: the concrete value assigned to the variable, and that value’s type descriptor. To be more precise, the value is the underlying concrete data item that implements the interface and the type describes the full type of that item. For instance, after
sourceRefs.textQuote: A variable of interface type stores a pair: the concrete value assigned to the variable, and that value’s type descriptor. To be more precise, the value is the underlying concrete data item that implements the interface

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var r io.Reader tty, err := os.OpenFile("/dev/tty", os.O_RDWR, 0) if err != nil { return nil, err } r = tty
sourceRefs.textQuote: var r io.Reader tty, err := os.OpenFile("/dev/tty", os.O_RDWR, 0) if err != nil { return nil, err } r = tty

sourceRefs.fallbackText: r contains, schematically, the (value, type) pair, ( tty , *os.File ). Notice that the type *os.File implements methods other than Read ; even though the interface value provides access only to the Read method, the value inside carries all the type information about that value. That’s why we can do things like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: r contains, schematically, the (value, type) pair, ( tty , *os.File ). Notice that the type *os.File implements methods other than Read ; even though the interface value provides access only to the Read method, the value

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var w io.Writer w = r.(io.Writer)
sourceRefs.textQuote: var w io.Writer w = r.(io.Writer)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The expression in this assignment is a type assertion; what it asserts is that the item inside r also implements io.Writer , and so we can assign it to w . After the assignment, w will contain the pair ( tty , *os.File ). That’s the same pair as was held in r . The static type of the interface determines what methods may be invoked with an interface variable
sourceRefs.textQuote: The expression in this assignment is a type assertion; what it asserts is that the item inside r also implements io.Writer , and so we can assign it to w . After the assignment, w will contain the pair ( tty , *os.File )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Continuing, we can do this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Continuing, we can do this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var empty interface{} empty = w
sourceRefs.textQuote: var empty interface{} empty = w

sourceRefs.fallbackText: and our empty interface value empty will again contain that same pair, ( tty , *os.File ). That’s handy: an empty interface can hold any value and contains all the information we could ever need about that value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: and our empty interface value empty will again contain that same pair, ( tty , *os.File ). That’s handy: an empty interface can hold any value and contains all the information we could ever need about that value.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (We don’t need a type assertion here because it’s known statically that w satisfies the empty interface. In the example where we moved a value from a Reader to a Writer , we needed to be explicit and use a type assertion because Writer ’s methods are not a subset of Reader ’s.)
sourceRefs.textQuote: (We don’t need a type assertion here because it’s known statically that w satisfies the empty interface. In the example where we moved a value from a Reader to a Writer , we needed to be explicit and use a type assertion

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One important detail is that the pair inside an interface variable always has the form (value, concrete type) and cannot have the form (value, interface type). Interfaces do not hold interface values.
sourceRefs.textQuote: One important detail is that the pair inside an interface variable always has the form (value, concrete type) and cannot have the form (value, interface type). Interfaces do not hold interface values.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Now we’re ready to reflect.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Now we’re ready to reflect.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: At the basic level, reflection is just a mechanism to examine the type and value pair stored inside an interface variable. To get started, there are two types we need to know about in package reflect : Type and Value . Those two types give access to the contents of an interface variable, and two simple functions, called reflect.TypeOf and reflect.ValueOf , r
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: At the basic level, reflection is just a mechanism to examine the type and value pair stored inside an interface variable. To get started, there are two types we need to know about in package reflect : Type and Value . T

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s start with TypeOf :
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s start with TypeOf :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: package main import ( "fmt" "reflect" ) func main() { var x float64 = 3.4 fmt.Println("type:", reflect.TypeOf(x)) }
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: package main import ( "fmt" "reflect" ) func main() { var x float64 = 3.4 fmt.Println("type:", reflect.TypeOf(x)) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This program prints
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This program prints

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You might be wondering where the interface is here, since the program looks like it’s passing the float64 variable x , not an interface value, to reflect.TypeOf . But it’s there; as godoc reports , the signature of reflect.TypeOf includes an empty interface:
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: You might be wondering where the interface is here, since the program looks like it’s passing the float64 variable x , not an interface value, to reflect.TypeOf . But it’s there; as godoc reports , the signature of refle

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // TypeOf returns the reflection Type of the value in the interface{}. func TypeOf(i interface{}) Type
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: // TypeOf returns the reflection Type of the value in the interface{}. func TypeOf(i interface{}) Type

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When we call reflect.TypeOf(x) , x is first stored in an empty interface, which is then passed as the argument; reflect.TypeOf unpacks that empty interface to recover the type information.
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: When we call reflect.TypeOf(x) , x is first stored in an empty interface, which is then passed as the argument; reflect.TypeOf unpacks that empty interface to recover the type information.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The reflect.ValueOf function, of course, recovers the value (from here on we’ll elide the boilerplate and focus just on the executable code):
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The reflect.ValueOf function, of course, recovers the value (from here on we’ll elide the boilerplate and focus just on the executable code):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var x float64 = 3.4 fmt.Println("value:", reflect.ValueOf(x).String())
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: var x float64 = 3.4 fmt.Println("value:", reflect.ValueOf(x).String())

sourceRefs.fallbackText: value: <float64 Value>
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: value: <float64 Value>

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (We call the String method explicitly because by default the fmt package digs into a reflect.Value to show the concrete value inside. The String method does not.)
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: (We call the String method explicitly because by default the fmt package digs into a reflect.Value to show the concrete value inside. The String method does not.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Both reflect.Type and reflect.Value have lots of methods to let us examine and manipulate them. One important example is that Value has a Type method that returns the Type of a reflect.Value . Another is that both Type and Value have a Kind method that returns a constant indicating what sort of item is stored: Uint , Float64 , Slice , and so on. Also methods
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Both reflect.Type and reflect.Value have lots of methods to let us examine and manipulate them. One important example is that Value has a Type method that returns the Type of a reflect.Value . Another is that both Type a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var x float64 = 3.4 v := reflect.ValueOf(x) fmt.Println("type:", v.Type()) fmt.Println("kind is float64:", v.Kind() == reflect.Float64) fmt.Println("value:", v.Float())
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: var x float64 = 3.4 v := reflect.ValueOf(x) fmt.Println("type:", v.Type()) fmt.Println("kind is float64:", v.Kind() == reflect.Float64) fmt.Println("value:", v.Float())

sourceRefs.fallbackText: type: float64 kind is float64: true value: 3.4
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: type: float64 kind is float64: true value: 3.4

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are also methods like SetInt and SetFloat but to use them we need to understand settability, the subject of the third law of reflection, discussed below.
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are also methods like SetInt and SetFloat but to use them we need to understand settability, the subject of the third law of reflection, discussed below.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The reflection library has a couple of properties worth singling out. First, to keep the API simple, the “getter” and “setter” methods of Value operate on the largest type that can hold the value: int64 for all the signed integers, for instance. That is, the Int method of Value returns an int64 and the SetInt value takes an int64 ; it may be necessary to con
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The reflection library has a couple of properties worth singling out. First, to keep the API simple, the “getter” and “setter” methods of Value operate on the largest type that can hold the value: int64 for all the signe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var x uint8 = 'x' v := reflect.ValueOf(x) fmt.Println("type:", v.Type()) // uint8. fmt.Println("kind is uint8: ", v.Kind() == reflect.Uint8) // true. x = uint8(v.Uint()) // v.Uint returns a uint64.
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: var x uint8 = 'x' v := reflect.ValueOf(x) fmt.Println("type:", v.Type()) // uint8. fmt.Println("kind is uint8: ", v.Kind() == reflect.Uint8) // true. x = uint8(v.Uint()) // v.Uint returns a uint64.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The second property is that the Kind of a reflection object describes the underlying type, not the static type. If a reflection object contains a value of a user-defined integer type, as in
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The second property is that the Kind of a reflection object describes the underlying type, not the static type. If a reflection object contains a value of a user-defined integer type, as in

sourceRefs.fallbackText: type MyInt int var x MyInt = 7 v := reflect.ValueOf(x)
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: type MyInt int var x MyInt = 7 v := reflect.ValueOf(x)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the Kind of v is still reflect.Int , even though the static type of x is MyInt , not int . In other words, the Kind cannot discriminate an int from a MyInt even though the Type can.
headingPath: 1. Reflection goes from interface value to reflection object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: the Kind of v is still reflect.Int , even though the static type of x is MyInt , not int . In other words, the Kind cannot discriminate an int from a MyInt even though the Type can.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Like physical reflection, reflection in Go generates its own inverse.
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Like physical reflection, reflection in Go generates its own inverse.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Given a reflect.Value we can recover an interface value using the Interface method; in effect the method packs the type and value information back into an interface representation and returns the result:
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Given a reflect.Value we can recover an interface value using the Interface method; in effect the method packs the type and value information back into an interface representation and returns the result:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: // Interface returns v's value as an interface{}. func (v Value) Interface() interface{}
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: // Interface returns v's value as an interface{}. func (v Value) Interface() interface{}

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As a consequence we can say
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: As a consequence we can say

sourceRefs.fallbackText: y := v.Interface().(float64) // y will have type float64. fmt.Println(y)
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: y := v.Interface().(float64) // y will have type float64. fmt.Println(y)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: to print the float64 value represented by the reflection object v .
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: to print the float64 value represented by the reflection object v .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We can do even better, though. The arguments to fmt.Println , fmt.Printf and so on are all passed as empty interface values, which are then unpacked by the fmt package internally just as we have been doing in the previous examples. Therefore all it takes to print the contents of a reflect.Value correctly is to pass the result of the Interface method to the f
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: We can do even better, though. The arguments to fmt.Println , fmt.Printf and so on are all passed as empty interface values, which are then unpacked by the fmt package internally just as we have been doing in the previou

sourceRefs.fallbackText: fmt.Println(v.Interface())
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: fmt.Println(v.Interface())

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (Since this article was first written, a change was made to the fmt package so that it automatically unpacks a reflect.Value like this, so we could just say
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: (Since this article was first written, a change was made to the fmt package so that it automatically unpacks a reflect.Value like this, so we could just say

sourceRefs.fallbackText: for the same result, but for clarity we’ll keep the .Interface() calls here.)
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: for the same result, but for clarity we’ll keep the .Interface() calls here.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since our value is a float64 , we can even use a floating-point format if we want:
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since our value is a float64 , we can even use a floating-point format if we want:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: fmt.Printf("value is %7.1e\n", v.Interface())
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: fmt.Printf("value is %7.1e\n", v.Interface())

sourceRefs.fallbackText: and get in this case
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: and get in this case

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Again, there’s no need to type-assert the result of v.Interface() to float64 ; the empty interface value has the concrete value’s type information inside and Printf will recover it.
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Again, there’s no need to type-assert the result of v.Interface() to float64 ; the empty interface value has the concrete value’s type information inside and Printf will recover it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In short, the Interface method is the inverse of the ValueOf function, except that its result is always of static type interface{} .
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In short, the Interface method is the inverse of the ValueOf function, except that its result is always of static type interface{} .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Reiterating: Reflection goes from interface values to reflection objects and back again.
headingPath: 2. Reflection goes from reflection object to interface value.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Reiterating: Reflection goes from interface values to reflection objects and back again.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The third law is the most subtle and confusing, but it’s easy enough to understand if we start from first principles.
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The third law is the most subtle and confusing, but it’s easy enough to understand if we start from first principles.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here is some code that does not work, but is worth studying.
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here is some code that does not work, but is worth studying.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var x float64 = 3.4 v := reflect.ValueOf(x) v.SetFloat(7.1) // Error: will panic.
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: var x float64 = 3.4 v := reflect.ValueOf(x) v.SetFloat(7.1) // Error: will panic.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you run this code, it will panic with the cryptic message
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you run this code, it will panic with the cryptic message

sourceRefs.fallbackText: panic: reflect.Value.SetFloat using unaddressable value
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: panic: reflect.Value.SetFloat using unaddressable value

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The problem is not that the value 7.1 is not addressable; it’s that v is not settable. Settability is a property of a reflection Value , and not all reflection Values have it.
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The problem is not that the value 7.1 is not addressable; it’s that v is not settable. Settability is a property of a reflection Value , and not all reflection Values have it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The CanSet method of Value reports the settability of a Value ; in our case,
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The CanSet method of Value reports the settability of a Value ; in our case,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: var x float64 = 3.4 v := reflect.ValueOf(x) fmt.Println("settability of v:", v.CanSet())
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: var x float64 = 3.4 v := reflect.ValueOf(x) fmt.Println("settability of v:", v.CanSet())

sourceRefs.fallbackText: settability of v: false
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: settability of v: false

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It is an error to call a Set method on a non-settable Value . But what is settability?
headingPath: 3. To modify a reflection object, the value must be settable.
sourceRefs.textQuote: It is an error to call a Set method on a non-settable Value . But what is settability?

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Settability is a bit like addressability, but stricter. It’s the property that a reflection object can modify the actual storage that was used to create the reflection object. Settability is determined by whether the reflection object holds the original item. When we say

sourceRefs.fallbackText: were allowed to succeed, it would not update x , even though v looks like it was created from x . Instead, it would update the copy of x stored inside the reflection value and x itself would be unaffected. That would be confusing and useless, so it is illegal, and settability is the property used to avoid this issue.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is straightforward and familiar, and reflection works the same way. If we want to modify x by reflection, we must give the reflection library a pointer to the value we want to modify.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Reflection can be hard to understand but it’s doing exactly what the language does, albeit through reflection Types and Values that can disguise what’s going on. Just keep in mind that reflection Values need the address of something in order to modify what they represent.
